does: This paper identifies that safety mechanisms in large language models are localized primarily in middle-to-late transformer layers, and exploits this architectural property through a novel white-box jailbreak method called SABER. The technique works by introducing an artificial residual connection between two intermediate layers that bypasses the safety-critical layers, effectively circumventing alignment training. The method achieves 51% improvement over baseline jailbreak approaches on the HarmBench dataset while maintaining minimal perplexity degradation, demonstrating that safety alignment in LLMs can be substantially undermined through targeted architectural manipulation rather than adversarial prompting alone.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining vulnerabilities in aligned language models, following earlier jailbreak studies that revealed limitations in RLHF-based safety training. It extends prior white-box attack research that analyzes internal model representations and layer-wise contributions to decision-making. The paper connects to interpretability work studying how safety mechanisms are distributed across transformer layers, building on findings that safety features concentrate in specific architectural regions. It also relates to broader research on adversarial attacks against neural networks and the robustness of learned constraints, particularly studies questioning whether alignment procedures truly teach robust safety versus surface-level compliance.
enables: This research points toward several future directions in both adversarial attack and defense mechanisms. It motivates the development of more robust alignment methods that distribute safety mechanisms across all layers rather than concentrating them in middle-late regions, potentially through techniques like layer-wise safety auditing or adversarial training against structural attacks. The findings enable more rigorous evaluation frameworks for safety alignment that account for architectural vulnerabilities. The work also establishes foundations for exploring whether similar layer-wise manipulation patterns apply across different model families and architectures, potentially leading to systematic vulnerability characterization. It opens questions about whether current alignment procedures fundamentally address safety or merely modify surface behavior.
